title: When the first answer isn't good enough

spec.title: When the first answer isn't good enough
spec.notes_for_generator: Core mechanic: deliberately get a mediocre first output, then fix it in one follow-up prompt. The lesson is that iteration is expected and desirable, not a sign of failure. Don't give away the follow-up prompt — make the reader figure out what specifically needs fixing. The coaching note should make explicit that one targeted follow-up prompt almost always outperforms trying to write a perfect first prompt.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 7: The First Draft Is Supposed to Be Mediocre

Most people assume a bad first output means they did something wrong. It doesn't. It means the workout has started.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
✍️ CREATION | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 12–18 min

THE BRIEF:
You need to write a short email to a client explaining a delay — something has slipped, the timeline is moving, and they need to hear it professionally. The email should acknowledge the situation, give a reason without over-explaining, and set clear next steps. Your job today isn't to write a perfect first prompt. It's to write a decent one, get a mediocre result, and then fix it with one targeted follow-up.

YOUR REPS:

1. Start with a straightforward prompt. Tell the AI you need a client email about a delay, and give it the basic facts: what's delayed, roughly by how long, and what you're doing about it. Keep your prompt simple — two or three sentences of context. Don't overthink it. Send it and see what comes back.

2. Read the output carefully. Be honest about what's wrong with it. Common issues to look for: it's too long, too apologetic, too vague about next steps, sounds like a template, or buries the key information. Pick the *single most important problem* and write one follow-up prompt that targets only that. Not a complete rewrite instruction — one specific fix. Compare the two outputs side by side.

3. Now look at what changed. Did fixing one thing improve the overall email, or did it create a new problem? If the email still isn't right, write one more follow-up targeting the next issue. Keep going until it's something you'd actually send. Notice how many rounds it took.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The final email is short enough that a client reads it fully — aim for under 150 words
• It contains exactly one clear next step, not a vague promise to "keep you updated"
• The tone sits between overly apologetic and defensively corporate — it sounds like a person wrote it
• You got there through iteration, not by rewriting your initial prompt from scratch

💡 COACHING NOTE:
One targeted follow-up almost always outperforms a more elaborate first prompt. Here's why: you can't know what's wrong with an output before you've seen it. Trying to anticipate every problem in your initial prompt leads to bloated, over-specified prompts that the AI struggles to follow. Instead, treat the first output as a diagnostic — it shows you exactly what needs fixing. Then you can fix that one thing cleanly. This principle transfers everywhere: summarisation, data analysis, presentation structure. The workflow is always draft, assess, target, refine.

──────────
Halfway through the free block, the point is not that you can write better prompts. It is that you are becoming the person at work who can think, draft, structure, and recover faster with AI. If you already know you want to keep building that, The 90-Day AI Habit is the paid next step: join solo, bring a small group, or get your employer to pay.

Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
